hard.next_step: JRA 6/29: BitSight's final decision has been delayed as a result of new CEO coming onboard. Positioning a call to discuss GEP, and how we differentiate on service/support.
hard.ais_why: AIS 3 / Negative: on the 16 Jan demo the only AI-adjacent action item was Amy Snider's internal check on whether legal uses a CLM, and BitSight self-rated its RFP AI need at the lowest non-zero tier (Basic Automation, rule based). Selection is being driven by demo quality and dashboard visibility, not AI capability.

north_star_verdict.headline: Live but frozen: BitSight has paused the P2P decision for an incoming CEO, no economic buyer or broader team has engaged since the 16 Jan demo, and the 29 Jul close (23 days out) is not credible. The deal needs re-qualification against new leadership before it is worth working hard.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 7 Jan 2026, in Shortlisted with close 29 Jul 2026 (23 days out as of 6 Jul). Deepest two-way buyer engagement was the 16 Jan demo (~5.7 months ago); the most recent genuine buyer signal was Amy Snider confirming confidence in the demos on 11 May. The 22 Jun task and 29 Jun next step are SELLER-side touches awaiting a buyer reply, not two-way engagement. The decision is explicitly delayed by an incoming new CEO; no down-selection, no EB, no legal/GRC/IT/FP&A engagement, no contract/SOW discussion. A Shortlisted deal cannot reach close in 23 days with no broader team assembled and a leadership transition in flight.
evidence: Next Step (29 Jun 2026, Justin Ajmo): final decision delayed as a result of a new CEO coming onboard; rep positioning a GEP service/support differentiation call.

Demo call 16 Jan 2026 (Amy Snider): plan was to review all vendor demos through the month, down-select by the third week of February, then assemble the broader team (legal, FP&A, AP); no evidence that team has since engaged.
Discovery call 14 Jan 2026 (Amy Snider): procurement is just Amy and Bernardo, so the evaluation is single-threaded at Director level.
Forecast is Upside Key Deal but no EB is engaged, no champion is driving a switch, and the buyer has paused the decision: the number is not defensible at this date.

metrics.narrative: The business problem is clear and qualitative but never quantified. On the 14 Jan 2026 discovery call Amy Snider named GEP platform reliability as the decisive issue, describing 'constant bugs and glitches that we are dealing with every single week' that force repeated support tickets. She wants auto-PO creation from executed contracts to remove manual re-keying, a contract-renewal dashboard to replace Excel tracking of 15+ monthly renewals, and a self-service supplier-onboarding intake she can edit without vendor help. None of this is tied to a dollar, headcount, or cycle-time figure, so there is no ROI model on record. Quantifying the cost of the reliability problem and the hours saved by automation is the open move.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer is engaged. Amy Snider, Global Director of Procurement, is the only buyer-side voice across both transcribed calls, and on a $130k platform replacement a Director cannot sign alone. She referenced executive backing for the original GEP rollout, which signals an exec layer exists above her but has not been brought into this evaluation. The 29 Jun next step confirms the most senior signal is now an incoming new CEO whose arrival has PAUSED the decision, so the budget authority for this deal is effectively unresolved until leadership settles. The path to power runs through Amy, not around her: she authored the GEP frustration and ran the 2022 selection, so arming her to broker the new-leadership conversation is the correct play, not cold outreach. Confirming who owns the P2P budget under the new CEO is the ranked move.
sources: SF OpportunityContactRole (Amy Snider, Decision Maker, primary)

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are well surfaced from the two January calls and centre on reliability and usability, not AI. Amy Snider's decisive criterion is platform stability: on 14 Jan, asked what one thing must be better, she said 'Reliability in the platform and in the product for sure.' Concrete functional criteria demonstrated and validated on the 16 Jan demo were a renewals dashboard to replace Excel, AI contract discovery / clause comparison against BitSight's own MSA, a Microsoft Word redline plugin so legal keeps its current workflow, auto-PO creation from an executed contract, Slack-based Merlin intake, DocuSign integration, and self-service configurability of intake questions and approvers. The AIS note confirms selection is being driven by 'demo quality and dashboard visibility, not AI capability,' so AI depth is a low-weight criterion here.

decision_process.narrative: The buyer's process was described explicitly on the 16 Jan demo by Amy Snider: BitSight would spend that month reviewing all vendor demos, shortlist and notify vendors by the third week of February, and only then bring in the broader stakeholder group (legal, FP&A, and AP) for a deeper, contract-data-specific presentation before deciding. That broader team has NOT engaged in the five-plus months since, and the 29 Jun next step reveals why: the final decision has been delayed by an incoming CEO. So the process is real and buyer-authored but has stalled at the down-select gate pending a leadership transition. The approval chain above Amy (and any procurement sign-off thresholds) is not yet on record.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are unknown after ~5.7 months. There is no discussion of MSA/SOW, redlines, procurement channel, or InfoSec for a Zycus purchase. GEP is the incumbent integrated with NetSuite (BitSight's ERP), and POs live in GEP today, so any switch implies an integration/migration scope that has never been touched. BitSight runs supplier risk reviews inside its own product (it sells TPRM), which simplifies one InfoSec track but leaves ERP/NetSuite integration as the real paper-and-scoping question. The incumbent GEP contract expiry, the natural forcing function, is not captured. The new-CEO transition means even a willing buyer cannot run paper until leadership settles, so paper is not the live constraint yet; re-qualification is. Capturing the GEP renewal date would surface any compelling event.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is specific, confirmed, and owned by Amy Snider's two-person procurement team. Primary pain: GEP platform instability, 'constant bugs and glitches every single week' generating repeated support tickets. Operational pain: she tracks 15+ monthly contract renewals on Excel because GEP gives her no renewal dashboard; she and Bernardo Silva must manually tag each other on every contract to share visibility; PO creation after a contract executes is fully manual; and changing intake-question wording requires waiting on the vendor. She uses ChatGPT outside the system to diff renewal contract clauses, signalling an unmet automation need. The pain owner is Amy and Bernardo; downstream legal and AP friction is implied but not directly confirmed.

champion.narrative: Amy Snider is a developing, well-engaged coach but not yet a switch-driving champion. Positives: she shaped both January calls, articulated pain candidly, signalled genuine openness to change, and ran the original 2022 GEP selection so she carries internal procurement authority. What is missing: she is single-threaded (Bernardo Silva is the only other procurement person, and he was absent on 14 Jan), she has not introduced an Economic Buyer or assembled the broader team she promised, and she carries the political weight of having chosen GEP herself, so reversing it is awkward for her. Her engagement has cooled into a rep-initiated holding pattern since 16 Jan. The conversion play is to give her reliability proof plus a quantified case so she can sell the switch internally, and to ask her directly whether the incoming CEO keeps P2P on the agenda.
sources: SF OpportunityContactRole (Amy Snider, Decision Maker, primary)

competition.narrative: GEP Worldwide is the entrenched incumbent and the deal to beat: BitSight selected it ~4 years ago (2022), it is integrated with NetSuite, has strong adoption, and runs the contract, supplier, PO, and eInvoicing stack today. The buyer's frustration is reliability, not function ('the platform's just not stable enough'), so the competitive wedge is stability plus AI-driven usability (Merlin Slack intake, auto-PO, renewal dashboards, Word redline sync). Coupa, iValua, JAGGAER, and SAP Ariba appear on the Salesforce competitor field as the broader shortlist but none was named by the buyer in the read calls, so they rank as lower/dormant current threats. The 29 Jun next step frames the live battle precisely: differentiate from GEP on service and support quality. The real near-term threat, however, is not a rival at all; it is the incoming CEO deprioritising the project and the status quo winning by inertia.

deal_movement.summary: No genuine buyer movement this sweep. The only activity is seller-side: a 22 Jun 'Call Attempted' task on the GEP-differentiation to-do and a 29 Jun rep next step recording that the decision is delayed for an incoming CEO. Close date has been pushed four times (28 Apr to 20 May to 18 Jun to 25 Jun to 29 Jul) and forecast slipped Best Case to Upside Key Deal on 19 May, both signs of a stalling, not advancing, deal.

items.change: Close date pushed 25 Jun to 29 Jul (4th slip since April)
Rep logged decision delayed by incoming new CEO; positioning a GEP service/support differentiation call
Rep 'Call Attempted' on the GEP-differentiation open commitment (awaiting buyer reply)
Forecast downgraded Best Case to Upside Key Deal

competitive_position.summary: GEP Worldwide is the single strongest current threat: the ~4-year incumbent selected by Amy Snider in 2022, NetSuite-integrated with strong adoption, where the only real gap is reliability per the 14 Jan call. Coupa, iValua, JAGGAER, and SAP Ariba sit on the Salesforce shortlist but were not named by the buyer in the read calls, so they rank as dormant current threats. We beat GEP by proving Zycus stability and a low-risk, AI-driven switch (Merlin, auto-PO, renewal dashboards, Word redline sync), but only if the new CEO keeps the project alive; the biggest real threat is do-nothing inertia, not a vendor.

competitors.quote: the platform's just not stable enough at all... constant bugs and glitches that we are dealing with every single week
competitors.how_we_win: Prove Zycus reliability with an uptime SLA, stability references, and an architecture comparison; position AI usability (Merlin Slack intake, auto-PO, renewal dashboards) as the low-disruption upgrade.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.how_we_win: Confirm whether Coupa is still in the evaluation; if active, differentiate on AI and service/support.

competitors.name: iValua
competitors.how_we_win: Verify presence in the shortlist; differentiate on integrated suite and usability.

competitors.name: JAGGAER
competitors.how_we_win: Verify presence in the shortlist; differentiate on AI-led adoption.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.how_we_win: Verify presence; position a simpler, more user-friendly platform vs SAP complexity.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: BitSight's core expectations (a renewals dashboard, AI clause discovery, a Word redline plugin that preserves legal's workflow, auto-PO from contract, Slack intake, DocuSign) were demonstrated and drew positive reactions on 16 Jan. The exposure is not fit but proof of reliability versus the incumbent and re-validation under new leadership.

best_practice_check.summary: Win-strategy priorities for a frozen, single-threaded challenger deal against an entrenched incumbent, ranked by leverage now.
flags: Re-qualify against the incoming CEO's priorities before investing more effort; get Amy to confirm whether P2P survives the leadership change and the revised timeline.
Break single-threading: the evaluation lives entirely with Amy and Bernardo; earn a second sponsor (legal or FP&A) so a champion loss or reorg does not kill the deal.
Arm Amy with reliability proof and a quantified instability-cost case so she can overcome the political awkwardness of reversing her own 2022 GEP choice.

items.name: Amy Snider
items.title: Global Director of Procurement
items.risk: Single-threaded; owns the 2022 GEP decision so reversing it is politically awkward; cannot approve $130k alone
items.source: SF OpportunityContactRole (primary); Avoma calls 14 & 16 Jan 2026; SF rep note 11 May 2026

items.name: Bernardo Silva
items.title: Accounts Payable Specialist
items.risk: Junior AP voice; engaged on the demo but not a decision-maker
items.source: SF OpportunityContactRole; Avoma demo call 16 Jan 2026

champion_strength.summary: Amy Snider is an engaged, candid coach but a developing champion: she shaped the discovery and demo and is openly frustrated with GEP, yet she is single-threaded, has not introduced an EB or assembled the broader team she promised, and carries the political weight of having selected GEP herself. Her advocacy has cooled into a rep-chased holding pattern since 16 Jan.
champion_strength.source: Avoma calls 14-16 Jan 2026; SF rep note 11 May 2026; SF Next Step 29 Jun 2026

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Merlin (Slack intake, AI contract discovery, clause comparison, obligation extraction) was demonstrated on 16 Jan and drew mild interest, but the buyer self-rated its RFP AI need at the lowest tier and selection is being driven by reliability and dashboards, not AI. The agentic story is not the wedge here; do not over-index on it.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 3 / Negative is corroborated by the calls: BitSight self-rated its RFP AI need at Basic Automation (rule based), and the only AI-adjacent demo action was an internal legal check. Amy does use ChatGPT outside the system to diff renewal clauses, a flicker of appetite, but AI is not a decision driver for this evaluation.

items.detail: No economic buyer engaged and the decision is frozen pending an incoming CEO; budget authority for the $130k switch is unresolved.
Amy Snider selected GEP in 2022, so championing a rip-and-replace of her own choice is internally awkward and slows her advocacy.
Do-nothing risk: BitSight keeps living with GEP's weekly glitches and never switches, a no-decision loss to status-quo inertia amplified by the leadership change, not a loss to another vendor.
Close date pushed four times since April to 29 Jul, which is not credible with the decision paused and no broader team assembled.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is cooling to dark: last genuine two-way buyer touch was Amy confirming demo confidence on 11 May, and the decision is now explicitly frozen for a new CEO. Recent activity is entirely seller-side.
items: Decision delayed pending incoming CEO (29 Jun 2026)
Four close-date slips since April with no buyer-driven progress
Broader team (legal, FP&A, AP) never engaged since the 16 Jan demo

deal_scores_evidence.summary: We are a shortlisted challenger to incumbent GEP with a candid Director-level coach in Amy Snider, but BitSight has frozen the P2P decision for an incoming CEO and no economic buyer or broader team has engaged since the 16 Jan demo; the one thing between us and the win is re-qualifying the deal under new leadership and giving Amy the reliability proof to overturn her own 2022 GEP choice.

win_position.text: Sits well below the Shortlisted cap of 70: a genuinely engaged Director coach and clear GEP reliability pain lift fit, but no economic buyer has ever engaged, the decision is frozen for a new CEO, and GEP is the 4-year incumbent Amy herself chose.

Real wedge exists: Amy Snider called GEP 'not stable enough... constant bugs and glitches every single week' (Avoma, 14 Jan), and every demoed capability (renewals dashboard, Word redline sync, auto-PO) landed positively on 16 Jan.
Single-threaded risk: the entire evaluation lives with Amy and Bernardo, so a reorg under the new CEO or a cooling champion can end the deal outright.
Do-nothing is the likeliest loss path: BitSight can keep tolerating GEP's glitches indefinitely, and the leadership change gives them every reason to defer.

deal_momentum.text: Momentum is negative: the close date has slipped four times since April and forecast dropped Best Case to Upside Key Deal on 19 May, with no buyer-driven event since the 16 Jan demo.
The only recent activity is a seller-side 22 Jun 'Call Attempted' and a 29 Jun rep note that the decision is delayed for the incoming CEO, both awaiting a buyer reply.

customer_commitment.text: Commitment has stalled: Amy promised on 16 Jan to shortlist by late February and bring in legal, FP&A and AP, but that broader team has never engaged and the decision is now explicitly paused.
The one positive commitment signal is Amy confirming on 11 May she was confident in the demos seen so far, but no Zycus-specific commercial next step followed.

deal_risk.text: Top risk is the leadership transition: an incoming CEO froze the decision (29 Jun), so budget ownership and project priority are unknown until they settle in.
Political drag: reversing the 2022 GEP choice is awkward for the only sponsor we have, and no exec above her has been reached.

close_plan_concretizing.evidence: Decision frozen for incoming CEO; close date slipped four times since April (SF field history + Next Step 29 Jun).

executive_alignment.evidence: No EB ever engaged; budget owner unknown under the new CEO (SF Next Step 29 Jun).

champion_coverage.evidence: Single-threaded on Amy and Bernardo; broader team never assembled (Avoma 16 Jan).

scope_change.detail: Scope has held steady since discovery: contract management, supplier onboarding, and eInvoicing with Merlin on the AI side, consistent with the 14 Jan discovery scoping; no modules added or dropped.

critical_signals.text: The P2P decision is frozen: BitSight has delayed its final choice because a new CEO is coming onboard, and no buyer-facing progress has happened since the January demo.
An incoming CEO now controls whether this project proceeds, and we have never met them and hold no relationship above the procurement director.
Our path is displacing the 4-year incumbent GEP on reliability and AI usability, but the buyer chose GEP herself, so the real threat is status-quo inertia rather than a rival.
The $130k Upside Key Deal number is not defensible: no budget owner is engaged and no pricing or commercial conversation has started.

customer_preference.evidence: Amy Snider confirmed confidence in the demos seen so far on 11 May and reacted positively to the renewals dashboard, Word redline sync and auto-PO on 16 Jan, but no Zycus-specific commercial next step followed and the decision is paused.

business_case.evidence: Qualitative pain only (GEP instability, manual PO creation, Excel renewal tracking for 15+ monthly renewals); no cost, savings, or cycle-time figure has been quantified.

items.action: Ask Amy whether the incoming CEO keeps P2P a priority and get the revised timeline.
items.trigger: Next step notes the final decision is delayed by a new CEO coming onboard.
items.expected_effect: Re-qualifies a frozen deal and replaces a dead 29 Jul date with a real one, or exposes it as a no-decision.

items.action: Send Amy a GEP service/support differentiation one-pager plus two reliability reference customers.
items.trigger: Rep's own next step is to position how Zycus differentiates from GEP on service and support.
items.expected_effect: Arms the champion to defend a switch and directly attacks the incumbent's only weakness.

items.action: Build a quantified instability-cost and automation-savings model with Amy's ticket and renewal volumes.
items.trigger: Pain is vivid but never quantified; no ROI case exists to justify the $130k switch.
items.expected_effect: Creates the business case Amy needs to sell a new CEO on displacing GEP.

items.action: Ask Amy to introduce one broader-team sponsor in legal or FP&A once leadership settles.
items.trigger: Amy promised legal, FP&A and AP would join at down-select but none has engaged.
items.expected_effect: Breaks single-threading so a reorg or champion loss does not kill the deal.

gaps: No buyer call since the 16 Jan 2026 demo; latest buyer signal is a rep-logged 11 May confidence note, so current-state evidence is thin.
Economic buyer / budget owner unnamed and unengaged, now unresolved under an incoming CEO.
Broader evaluation team (legal, FP&A, AP) never engaged.
Incumbent GEP contract expiry and any compelling event unknown.
